sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Dyspnoea refers to the sensation of shortness of breath or difficulty in breathing. It can be a subjective experience, meaning that it is based on the individual's perception of their breathing difficulty. Dyspnoea can range from mild to severe distress and can occur during rest or physical activity.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Distressed, anxious, fighting to breath, exhausted, altered level of consciousness
- Pale, sweaty and can be cyanosed
- Single words responses or unable to speak
- Stridor, crackles, wheeze, silent chest
- Breathing rate of < 6 or > 20
- Accessory muscle use, intercostal recession and/or tracheal tug, prolonged expiratory phases
- Tachycardia > 120 or bradycardia < 60

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Hypoxic aggression / irritability
- Oxygen rejection may be due to hypoxia
- Never cease oxygen administration if patient's level of consciousness or breathing deteriorates; oxygen almost never depresses breathing in the field and can usually be attributed exhaustion and fatigue (especially of the diaphragm). Exhaustion can depress breathing and/or level of consciousness, even if the patient is given oxygen
- Whilst hyperventilation is usually associated with severe emotional stress, do not over- diagnose "hyperventilation" in the field. Your patient could have a pulmonary embolus or other serious problem
- ACS can manifest as dyspnoea and may be the only indication of an acute myocardial infarction, therefore the need for a 12-Lead ECG should be considered
- Oedematous upper airway of rapid onset and airway obstruction due to neck trauma have a high potential to evolve into complete airway obstruction. Neck trauma can cause rapid oedema and complete airway obstruction
- Partial airway obstruction may progress to complete obstruction. Limit interventions to only those essential to maintain oxygenation. Always prepare for the management of a complete obstruction
- Causes to dyspnoea to consider are airway obstruction, cardiovascular compromise, neurological causes, respiratory compromise, thoracic musculoskeletal compromise

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Continuous reassurance
- Treat airway obstruction
- If respiratory distress is caused by exposure to any hazardous substance, remove substance or patient if safe to do so. Assistance from site-based Emergency Response Teams, Emergency Service (DFES) or other appropriate resource may be required
- Position patient appropriately (sitting upright or in a position of comfort; lateral position if unconscious, consider appropriate position in suspected spinal injury)
- Conduct Vital Sign Survey, particularly respiratory rate and pulse oximetry
- Consider oxygen therapy and titrate to clinical need
- Ventilate the patient if required
- Auscultate for diagnostic clues: wheeze, crackles, pleuritic rub, silent chest
- Limb lead ECG
- Consider Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Manage underlying condition if known
- Wheeze/silent chest: Asthma guideline
- Crackles/Pleuritic rub: Chest Infection guideline
- Pain/Trauma: Pain Management / Chest Trauma guideline
- Consider advanced Airway if indicated
- Consider IV access
- 12 lead ECG
